answer:
Sketch:
- 17 U.S.C. §412: statutory damages/attorney's fees need registration before infringement OR within 3 months of first publication
- Published mid-Jan 2026 → grace window closed ~mid-April 2026
- Now July → too late for this infringer if piracy began before you register
- Ongoing infringement counts as "commenced" at first act — no reset
- Registration still required to file suit at all (Fourth Estate, 2019)

Answer: Probably not for this pirate site. Under §412, statutory damages and attorney's fees are only available if you registered before the infringement began or within 3 months of first publication. Your 3-month grace window closed around mid-April, and courts treat ongoing infringement as commencing at the first infringing act — so if the site started distributing before you register, statutory damages are off the table for it. You'd be limited to actual damages plus the infringer's profits, which are often hard to prove against pirate sites.

Register now anyway: (1) you can't file an infringement suit at all without a registration (Fourth Estate v. Wall-Street.com), and (2) any new infringers who start after registration will be exposed to statutory damages up to $150,000 for willful infringement. In the meantime, send a DMCA takedown to the site's host — that requires no registration and is usually the fastest practical remedy. One caveat worth checking with counsel: if the pirating actually started after you register, statutory damages would be available, so pin down when the infringement began.
